sentences.text: My full name is Ellen Brandt.
E-L-L-E-N B-R-A-N-D-T.
Ellen Ruth Friedsam.
Want me to spell the last name?
Ellen Ruth, I think is OK.
F-R-I-E-D-S-A-M.
Place of birth is Mannheim, Germany.
Well, I was born in a hospital in Mannheim, because the hospital in the town where my mother came from, Landau, where my parents resided at the time, wasn't good enough for my grandfather.
So I was born in Mannheim.
I was there for two weeks.
We moved to Munich when I was six months old.
My father had bought a factory, a paper factory.
And I lived in Munich until the first of January, 1933, when we moved to Berlin.
My father saw the handwriting on the wall, and we moved to Berlin.
Well, it was an upscale neighborhood.
I had my own-- not nanny.
I had my own nanny.
I was an only child.
My mother was deemed too old to have another child.
I had a very comfortable life.
I-- my father was very, very strict.
I was not a spoiled single child.
I decided I wanted to be an actress when I was about five years old.
I made all the neighborhood children act in plays with me, and wrote my first play when I was six.
And charged the parents five pfennig to attend all these little plays.
And I think there's one interesting story that I should tell.
I had a very, very proverbial rich uncle in America, my grandmother's brother.
We were then living in a building.
My grandparents had moved to Munich because both their daughters were married and lived in Munich.
And the proverbial rich uncle from America came with his entourage.
They took an entire floor in the Four Seasons Hotel in Munich, which was probably one of three four, five-star hotels in Europe at the time.
They took an entire floor.
They came with an entourage of 10, 12.
And that year, my uncle brought with him his general manager, a Mr.
Wilson.
And my uncle saw how well my father was doing with his factory.
And he said to my father, listen.
I just bought a new factory in Cleveland.
Why don't you move to America?
Get out of Germany.
Move to America and become president of the Cleveland foundry.
And my father thought about it.
And this Mr.
Wilson, who was not a Jew, took my father aside and said, Herr Friedsam, you would be crazy to move to America.
In America, we have a thing called anti-Semitism.
You know nothing about anti-Semitism in Germany.
You are a high-ranking-- you are 32nd degree Mason.
Half of your friends are non-Jews.
You have a very good life here.
There is no anti-Semitism in America-- there is no anti-Semitism in Germany, in Munich.
You have a six-year-old daughter.
Someday she'll want to go to college.
In America, she would have to belong to a Jewish sorority, because she would not be accepted in the regular sororities.
You could never be a Freemason in America.
They wouldn't take you as a Jew.
You stay in Germany.
This was 1928, and it was one of the worst decisions my father ever made.
Also, another interesting thing about those years was that Hitler lived one block away from us.
There was an opera house at the head of [GERMAN] Prinzregentenstrasse, where we lived.
And he had a rented room.
And he walked past my grandparents' home every single day on his way to and from work.
And if anyone had known what was going to happen, my father would have gone up on the roof, taken a gun-- he was a big shot in World War I.
He was a much decorated hero.
He would have taken a gun and changed the course of history.
And he would have had total protection from his regimental commanders.
For instance, we were living in Munich in 1923, during the putsch when Hitler first tried to take command, which landed him in jail.
And the night before the putsch, my father got a phone call saying, Herr Friedsam, tomorrow, you stay home.
Click.
And of course, that's all my father had to hear.
He was up at dawn, and he was right in the middle of everything the whole day.
He didn't look Jewish.
Nobody to took him for a Jew.
And he watched the entire-- the shooting, Ludendorff, Hitler, the entire history of the day.
Until the very, very last day before we left Germany in 1938, my father periodically would get telephone calls saying, tomorrow you stay home.
He never did.
Never once.
I don't remember too much about those years.
I was too little.
I do remember one incident when I went to play with my three best friends.
We lived on four corners of an intersection.
And it was end of November, beginning of December, just before Saint Nicholas Day.
And we were going to start on our Christmas decorations.
And one of the little six-year-olds-- we were in first grade-- one of the little six-year-olds turned to me and said, you can't play with us this year.
And I said, why?
And it turned out that they had been told by their parents that I was Jewish.
And this was before Hitler came to power.
And I went running home crying to my parents.
And I found other friends.
I never knew-- as long as we lived in Munich-- I never had any idea which of my parents' friends were Jewish, which of my friends were Jewish.
It was all one and the same thing.
Life was easy.
My father was doing very well, running a factory.
And we went away every single weekend.

My parents played four-handed piano every afternoon when the weather was inclement, which was a lovely story, because every afternoon my parents would sit down at the piano bench and kiss passionately.
Because they knew that by the end of half an hour of playing four-handed piano, my father would be yelling at my mother, because when it said pianissimo, my father played softly and slowly.
And when it said forte, my father would be loud and fast.
And my mother was very, very steady, like a metronome.
And my father would always yell and say, back to K, back to M.
And then they would end up my mother in tears and my father yelling.
And the next day, they would sit down and kiss again passionately and start it all over again.
As a family, children weren't taken anywhere.
Nothing.
Nothing.
My parents gave a big party once a year, for instance, for Fasching, which is Mardi Gras, which is a big deal in Munich, which is a Catholic city.
Every piece of furniture was taken out of the apartment.
I was shipped upstairs to my grandparents' apartment.
We had like, 14 rooms, and to put furniture in storage just for a party.
There were booths set up.
I mean, it was a pretty lavish lifestyle.
We went away every single weekend.
In winter, skiing, in summer, usually to Austria, Switzerland, the Alps.
My father always had a car.
And we went every single weekend.
My father had wonderful luck with weather.

My grandmother would be standing on the balcony upstairs, looking down, wringing her hands, saying 'thank god, you're back' every single Sunday night, because it had poured or snowed or hailed in Munich.
And wherever we were, the sun was shining.
It was a good, easy life.
My parents both played tennis every afternoon.
I-- the tennis courts-- I was deemed much too young to start tennis.
But I did start ice skating at the age of four.
The tennis courts were flooded.
And I started training for the Olympics when I was four.
I was put on skis when I was five.
I swam across a lake when I was nine.
My father was very unhappy that I was a girl, and therefore brought me up like a boy.
Well, I know a great deal about both of them.
My maternal grandparents came from Landau Pfalz, Palatinate.
And they had nothing but money.
My grandfather-- my grand-- great-grandparents were already very well to do.
My grandfather had vineyards and made wine, from growing the grape to bottling the wine.
He was quite well to do.
My father's family was the interesting family.
Their name-- his name was [NON-ENGLISH] and my grandmother's name was [NON-ENGLISH] from Landau Pfalz.
My father's family was the interesting family.

When Hitler came out with one of his endless edicts that every German schoolchild had to make up an ancestral tree, my father wrote one single letter to a little town on the Rhine called Friesem, F-R-I-E-S-E-M, where the family had originally come from.
When they moved to Koblenz, which was the big city on the Rhine that they lived in for a couple of hundred years, their name was changed from Friesem to Friedsam, which was customary for Jews.
They were all given names that had meaning, supposedly.
Friedsam means peaceful.
My father wrote a letter to the courthouse or the city hall of Friesem on the Rhine and explained that his family had come from there.
And would they please send any record that they had of how long the family had been there.
And he immediately, with German punctuality and correctness-- this in 1935, '36-- they wrote back a letter saying, we're terribly sorry.
We have no records that pre-date the year 1250, because city hall was burnt down in 1250.
But at that time, there definitely was a [?
Mitchell ?] Friedsam living in Friesem.
So my family had been in Germany since, certainly since the 13th century.
My-- the family was awarded a coat of arms about 200, 250 years ago, a bourgeois coat of arms that died out with my father.
There are very few of those.
My grandfather was Leopold Friedsam.
My grandmother's name was [NON-ENGLISH] was her maiden name.
None.
Very, very little.
As I say, I never even knew which of my friends or my parents' friends were Jewish and which were not.
It, it-- there really was very, very little anti-Semitism.
I knew that my father had encountered anti-Semitism in World War I, and therefore, had not become a general.
He went up as high as a Jew could go.
So I was very vaguely aware that, that Jews were-- we did not-- we celebrated Christmas and Easter.
The only observance that I know of was my father fasted on Yom Kippur.
I don't know why.
You know, it's the same old thing, I don't believe in God, but just in case.
He did observe Yom Kippur, but my parents did not observe any of the holidays.
We did not belong-- I don't even know where there was a synagogue or a temple in Munich.
I don't think so.
There are too many other important things that happened later.
We moved to Berlin on the 1st of January, 1933.
And as I always like to say, Hitler was so devastated that we moved that he followed us exactly one month later.
My father was offered a big job in Berlin, and he saw the handwriting on the wall.
We all knew that Hitler was-- that Hindenburg was a senile, old man.
The Kaiser was nothing.
And my father was aware of the fact that once Hitler took over, that we had to get out of Germany, and that it was very dangerous for anyone to be the owner of a factory.
And he took a job.
It was a very cushy job.
It paid a lot of money.
But it was a lot safer to be anonymous and to be working for an Aryan company, rather than to be owning a Jewish factory.
That was the reason we moved to Berlin.
Well, I lived in Wilmersdorf, which is West Berlin.
We lived in a typical Berlin four-story building, and I have a picture of it.
We had, I don't know, 10, 12-room apartment.
There were two apartments to each floor, an elevator landing.
And very conveniently, the other people who lived on the same floor as we did were also Jewish, which was wonderful.
There were three Jewish families out of eight.
And it curved around a courtyard, all the way to the servants' quarters in the back.
And we had, I don't know, a cook, and somebody for me, and a [GERMAN], I mean, a serving, cleaning girl.
And my father, of course, always had a car.
And we lived a comfortable life in the beginning.
Well, I was 10 years old.
I mean, it was not-- I was not aware of politics, per se.
I know, in retrospect, that from the time that Hitler took over in January of '33, there was no more freedom of speech.
You had to-- I mean, even as a 10-year-old, I was told that I had to watch every single word that I said outside of the home, as well as inside.
I think one of the more surprising things in the world to me in America when I first came was to find out that in America, people criticized the president.
My god.
It was totally unthinkable.
I don't think food rationing started in '33, but certainly there were some changes right from the beginning.
I think whipped cream was one of the first things disappeared from the face of the German Earth.
In the beginning, there were very few changes, other than freedom of speech.
I'll tell you something that happened in 1933.
We moved to Berlin and my father renewed his friendship with his best friend from Koblenz from his school days.
His name-- the man's name was Dr.
Kurt ?] [?
Singer. ?] He was then the [GERMAN], artistic director, as well as managing director, of the Staatsoper, which is the city opera of Berlin.
And he was married to the [?
Beverly ?] [?
Sills ?] of Berlin.
Her name was [?
Margret ?] [?
Pfahl. ?] She was blonde and blue-eyed.
She had divorced a husband.
He had divorced his wife.
He had become famous for starting the first-- he was a doctor.
He had studied medicine as had-- his father was a doctor.
His grandfather was a doctor.
He became bored with the practice of medicine, and he formed the first physician's orchestra in Berlin.
And it became such a great orchestra that he became the director of the city opera.
One of the very, very first things I remember about 1933 is that he had to divorce his wife, and they were madly in love with each other.
As a matter of fact, these two people met in my parents' bedroom until the day we left Germany.
I didn't know about that at the time, because I was conveniently in school.
But once a week, they met in my parents' bedroom.
And if anyone had ever caught any of them, they all would have been shot.
But [NON-ENGLISH] came with us to Denmark that summer while the divorce was in progress.
But Hitler immediately had decreed that this wonderful soprano, the darling of Berlin, blonde, blue-eyed [?
Margaret ?] [?
Pfahl, ?] had to divorce the Jew.
That was primarily all I remember about 1933.
People in America think that concentration camps didn't come into being until, god knows when, '38, '39, '40, which is so ridiculous.
The first concentration camp was opened in 1933.
In early 1934, a friend of my father's was the first person I knew who was killed in concentration camp.
The early concentration camps were primitive in comparison to what happened later.
Obviously, there were no gas ovens then.
It was a quarry.
He was a very, very wealthy man with a beautiful estate and a big factory.
His name was [GERMAN].
And they came and got him in the middle of the night, because some Nazi desired his home and another Nazi desired, coveted, this factory.
He was taken to this quarry.
And he was made to lift rocks and carry the rocks from one side of the quarry to the other side and back again.
And one day, he came upon a rock he couldn't lift.
And a man stood over him and kept hitting him until he finally dropped dead.
And in those days, the Germans were so civilized, they even sent his watch, and his ring, and his wallet back home to his wife.
They didn't bother with those things later on.
They took the gold rings along with the gold from the teeth and melted it down.

But I was aware, whether it was '33 or '34, that if you said the wrong thing to the wrong person in school, in front of a budding Nazi child who had been already indoctrinated by parents at home, that they would come and take your parents and shoot them.
I mean, those days, they didn't bother much.
They just bang, bang, bang and--
Well, I'll give you a wonderful example.
In about 1930-- my father's best friend in Berlin was assistant to Dr.
Sauerbruch, which is a name that would mean nothing to you, but a name that will mean everything to any German who ever lived in Germany.
Dr.
Sauerbruch was the DeBakey of his time.
He was the foremost surgeon in Europe.
And my father's best friend was his assistant.
And Sauerbruch, of course, became Hitler's personal physician, among other things.
He headed the big hospital in Berlin called the Charite, which still exists to this day.
It was in East Germany.
It's decrepit today.
A young doctor was getting married, not Jewish.
As a matter of fact, I don't think any of them were Jewish.
He had a stag party the night before his wedding.
And my father's best friend, [?
Willi ?] [?
Felix, ?] [?
Dr.
Felix, ?] was invited along with Dr.
Sauerbruch and all the top staff of the Charite.
And somebody told a silly, dirty story, a joke about Hitler.
The man was picked up at five o'clock the next morning, and no one ever heard from him again.
You couldn't tell funny stories, anything derogatory about Hitler.
It was drilled into us.
It was-- it-- you know, and with every passing year, of course, the drill became more and more intense.
It was unnecessary by that time.
We all knew.
Too many people disappeared.
We all knew about the knocks on the door in the middle of the night.
We all knew-- like we hear the stories about Chile and, and, and Argentina.
People disappear.
So that-- and no child wants to hurt their own parents.
I mean, that's a guilt trip that nobody needs, to be responsible for your parents' arrest.
Well, I will say something incredibly shocking.
My life was pretty good in Berlin.
Berlin was a marvelous city.
It's a wonderful city, cosmopolitan.
You could go by subway to a lake inside the city after school.
We had a car.
Money was a subject that was not mentioned in my home, because it was there.
In '36, one of the edicts-- or maybe even it was '35. '35 was the beginning of a different life.
The Nuremberg Laws came-- were published, and they defined our lives.
Jews could no longer be teachers, civil servants.
Jews could no longer appear on stage, perform any instrument in public.
